title: How euro area firms' inflation expectations affect their business decisions
summary: ECB staff analyzed new survey data from the Survey on the Access to Finance of Enterprises (SAFE) showing that euro area firms expected 5.8% inflation one year ahead in June 2023, higher than the 5.5% actual rate at the time, with longer-horizon expectations moderating to 5.0% (3-year) and 4.8% (5-year). A randomized controlled trial found that firms significantly revised their inflation expectations upon receiving professional forecaster data showing 2.8% one-year-ahead inflation, adjusting roughly halfway toward that figure, and that higher inflation expectations correlate with plans for larger price and wage increases.
key_points: Euro area firms' average 1-year-ahead inflation expectations were 5.8% in June 2023, compared to actual inflation of 5.5%
Firms' longer-horizon expectations moderated to 5.0% at 3 years and 4.8% at 5 years, still above ECB's 2% target
Firms on average expect higher inflation than professional forecasters but less than households, with greater dispersion than professional forecasters
Smaller, younger firms and those with higher debt ratios tend to have higher inflation expectations than larger, older, less-leveraged firms
In a randomized controlled trial, firms given ECB SPF forecast of 2.8% one-year-ahead inflation revised their expectations roughly halfway toward that figure
Higher inflation expectations correlate with firms' plans to increase prices and wages more substantially
Firms responded to new information despite the high-inflation environment, suggesting capacity to update expectations when provided with credible forecasts
why_it_matters: This research is relevant for monetary policy because it demonstrates that firms' inflation expectations—which influence pricing, wage bargaining, and investment decisions—are malleable and respond to credible information. The finding that firms revise expectations when presented with professional forecasts suggests communication can help anchor expectations. The dispersion in firm expectations, particularly at longer horizons, indicates potential for self-fulfilling dynamics if firms with elevated expectations act on them through wages and prices.
macro_context: This ECB research addresses a key transmission mechanism: if firms set prices and wages based on their inflation expectations, and those expectations are elevated relative to the 2% target, this could create persistence in inflation. The finding that expectations respond to new information suggests monetary policy communication has potential to influence actual outcomes through this channel. However, the source does not discuss direct market impact or rate path implications.
important_numbers: 5.5% - actual euro area inflation at time of survey
2.8% - ECB SPF forecast for 1-year-ahead inflation provided in information treatment
5-6% range - approximate adjustment range for firms given 11% prior expectation
50% - approximate degree of revision toward professional forecast

issues_found: Source body is truncated; final section on how inflation expectations affect business decisions is incomplete, preventing full verification of the key finding about price/wage plans

human_review.notes: Record is mostly accurate and well-structured. Minor concern is the truncated source body, but the record appropriately notes this limitation. The key claims about inflation expectations (5.8%, 5.0%, 4.8%, 2.8% figures), the RCT methodology, and the main findings are all verifiable and correctly stated from the available source text.

notes: Source is an ECB staff research blog post analyzing new SAFE survey data on firm inflation expectations. Relevant to macro catalysts through the inflation expectations channel. Historical record from July 2024; data reflects June 2023 survey period during elevated inflation. The randomized controlled trial methodology provides credible evidence that firms update expectations when presented with professional forecasts, but the source does not discuss near-term rate implications or market reactions. Truncation noted in source body; final paragraph on business decision implications may be incomplete.

reasoning: Substantive ECB research with empirical methodology
Direct relevance to macro catalysts through inflation expectations channel
Clear data points on firm expectations at multiple horizons
Notable RCT finding on expectation updating
However: historical data (June 2023), truncated content, no market impact discussion
Lower quality tier due to age and incomplete content
